title: Why your brain treats emotional pain the same as physical injury
author: Kalesh

The body doesn't know the difference. 

 You stub your toe. You feel a sharp, immediate jolt of pain. Your brain lights up in specific regions. Your body...
htmlContent:
The body doesn't know the difference.

You stub your toe. You feel a sharp, immediate jolt of pain. Your brain lights up in specific regions. Your body releases stress hormones. You might curse, grab your foot, maybe even tear up a little.

Now think about the last time someone said something that cut you deep. A partner's dismissal. A parent's criticism. A friend's betrayal. That same sharp jolt hit your chest, didn't it? Your stomach dropped. Your throat tightened. Maybe you felt like crying or punching a wall.

Here's the thing: your brain processes both of those experiences using many of the same neural circuits. The physical pain from your stubbed toe and the emotional pain from that cutting remark. They're not as different as we like to think.

This isn't some metaphor or poetic license. It's hard neuroscience. And understanding this changes everything about how we heal.

Naomi Eisenberger, a researcher at UCLA, has spent years studying social pain. She puts people in fMRI scanners and has them experience social rejection. What she found stopped the field cold. The same regions that light up when you burn your hand light up when you're excluded from a game. The dorsal anterior cingulate cortex. The anterior insula. These are pain processing centers. Pure and simple.

Your brain treats a broken heart like a broken bone. It really does. Stay with me here.

Why evolution built this system

Think about it from an evolutionary perspective. For our ancestors, being part of a tribe wasn't just nice. It was survival. Exile meant death. A saber-toothed tiger could eat you. You couldn't hunt alone. Your chances of surviving winter without the group were basically zero.

So nature needed a way to keep us connected. A way to make isolation feel terrible. A way to make rejection hurt so badly we'd do almost anything to avoid it.

That's what emotional pain is. It's a signal. A warning system. Your brain saying "something is wrong with your social connections and you need to fix it or you will die."

Wild, right? The same system that makes you yank your hand away from a hot stove is the system that makes you feel hollow after a breakup.

This is where Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory come in. Porges showed that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. Not just physical threats. Social threats too. A raised eyebrow. A cold tone. A turned back. Your vagus nerve picks up on these cues and shifts your entire physiological state.

When you feel emotionally hurt, your body doesn't just feel sad. It goes into a protective state. Your heart rate changes. Your breathing shallows. Your digestion slows. Muscles tense. You might feel frozen or agitated. This is your nervous system responding to what it perceives as a threat to your survival.

Because from your brain's perspective, it is.

The body keeps the score, even when you don't

Here's where it gets personal for me. I spent years thinking my emotional pain was all in my head. That I just needed to think differently. Reframe my experiences. Practice gratitude. Move on.

But my body wasn't getting that memo.

I'd have conversations that triggered old wounds and my shoulders would hike up to my ears. My jaw would clench. My stomach would knot up. I'd feel this dull ache in my chest that no amount of positive thinking could touch.

That's because emotional pain gets stored in the body. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote the landmark book on this subject, showed through decades of research that trauma and emotional suffering leave physical traces. The body remembers what the mind tries to forget.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He traces how suppressed emotional pain leads to real physical disease. Not because you're weak. Because your body was never designed to carry that weight alone.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago. She asked me where I felt a certain memory in my body. I thought she was crazy. But I closed my eyes and there it was. A tight band across my ribs. A burning sensation in my throat. That's where my grandfather's criticism lived. That's where my parents' divorce was stored. That's where every rejection I couldn't process had taken up residence.

Your body doesn't lie about emotional pain the way your mind can. Your mind can rationalize. Minimize. Dismiss. Your body just keeps the score.

What this means for healing

If emotional pain is processed like physical pain, then we need to treat it that way. You wouldn't tell someone with a broken leg to just think happy thoughts and walk it off. But that's exactly what we do to people with emotional wounds.

We tell them to get over it. To forgive and forget. To look on the bright side. We hand them platitudes wrapped in pretty paper and call it support.

Here's the reality: emotional pain requires real care. Physical care. The kind that addresses your nervous system, not just your thoughts.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium plays a role in regulating the nervous system and supporting the GABA receptors that help you calm down. It's not a cure. Nothing is. But it's a tool. A way to support your body while you do the harder work of healing.

What I've learned is that healing emotional pain requires the same things as healing physical pain. Rest. Safety. Gentle movement. Time. And sometimes, help from someone who knows what they're doing.

You need to let your nervous system know it's safe now. That the threat has passed. That you're not still being rejected or abandoned or hurt. But your nervous system doesn't speak English. It speaks sensation. It speaks rhythm. It speaks presence.

That's why somatic approaches work. That's why breathwork helps. That's why gentle movement, touch, and connection can shift things that talk therapy alone can't touch.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." That shift matters. Because when you understand that your emotional pain is a survival response, not a character flaw, you can start to meet it with compassion instead of shame.

The problem with pretending you're fine

We live in a culture that rewards emotional stoicism. Keep it together. Don't let them see you sweat. Fake it till you make it. We've built entire industries around bypassing pain instead of processing it.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. Levine developed Somatic Experiencing, a method that helps people complete the incomplete stress responses stuck in their bodies after trauma. He showed that animals in the wild don't get traumatized because they complete the cycle. They shake. They run. They discharge the energy. Humans suppress it. And that suppression becomes chronic pain, anxiety, and emotional suffering.

I've done this myself. Smiled through family gatherings while my stomach churned. Laughed at work while my chest felt like it was caving in. Told myself I was fine while my body screamed otherwise.

But here's what I've learned: your body will not be ignored forever. It will find a way to get your attention. Maybe through migraines. Maybe through back pain. Maybe through anxiety attacks at 3 AM. Maybe through a chronic illness that doctors can't explain.

The emotional pain you pretend isn't there will eventually speak. The question is whether you'll listen before it has to shout.

What actually helps

So what do you do with all this? How do you start treating emotional pain like the physical experience it actually is?

First, stop judging yourself for feeling it. That pain in your chest after a breakup? It's real. That hollow feeling after losing someone? It's not weakness. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. Honor that. Thank it for trying to protect you. Then start working with it instead of against it.

Second, learn to track your body. When you feel emotionally triggered, pause. Don't run to your thoughts. Drop into your body. Where do you feel it? What does it feel like? Tight? Hot? Cold? Heavy? Just noticing changes the relationship. You stop being the pain and start being the observer of the pain. That's the beginning of freedom.

Third, find ways to complete the stress response. Your body needs to know the threat is over. Movement helps. Shaking. Yawning. Sighing. Deep breathing. Cold water on your face. These are not woo-woo techniques. They're physiological interventions that signal safety to your nervous system.

Fourth, get support. You cannot heal relational wounds in isolation. We are wired for connection. The same system that makes rejection painful also makes attuned connection healing. Find a therapist who understands trauma and the body. Find friends who can sit with your pain without trying to fix it. Find community that doesn't demand you be okay.

I'm not going to tell you this is easy. It's not. I'm not going to tell you there's a quick fix. There isn't. Healing emotional pain takes time. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to feel things you've been avoiding for years.

But here's what I can tell you: it's possible. Your nervous system can learn new patterns. Your brain can rewire. The pain can soften. Not disappear completely, maybe. But soften enough that you can breathe again. Soften enough that you can feel joy without guilt. Soften enough that you can connect without fear.

Your emotional pain is not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that you're human. And treating it with the same care you'd give a physical wound? That's not weakness. That's wisdom.

Sit with that for a moment. Notice what just happened in your body as you read these words. That's your healing beginning.

  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate's exploration of stress and illness
